sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Christina, presents with a non-healing foot ulcer on her right foot, which has been present for approximately one year.
She reports that the wound initially started as a blister, became red and infected, and was treated with antibiotics, but has not fully resolved.
She denies any recent trauma to the foot.
The patient has a history of type 2 diabetes and reports having neuropathy, with decreased sensation in her foot.
She mentions that the wound has been producing more drainage lately and she has been feeling tired.
Past medical history: The patient has type 2 diabetes and has been experiencing neuropathy.
She has been taking Keflex antibiotics for the past five months.
Review of systems: The patient reports fatigue and increased drainage from the wound.
Current medications: Keflex [500mg] [twice daily] [for infection].
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has a 1 by 2 centimeter (2 inch) circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth MTP joint.
The wound has yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue, but no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, fluid collection, necrosis, or bony exposure.
The wound does not hurt when touched.
Test Results: X-ray of the right foot shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with possible decreased blood flow.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; decreased blood flow to the right foot; high A1c; uncontrolled type 2 diabetes.
Plan:
I prescribed colaganase ointment to be applied once a day to the wound, covered with a sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement of the wound in the office.
I ordered an ABI (ankle-brachial index) test to determine the patient's blood supply.
I will obtain a surgical shoe to reduce pressure on the area.
I instructed the patient to wear the surgical shoe and avoid putting pressure on the wound.
I will see the patient back in two weeks to reassess the wound and possible perform another debridement.
Follow-up: I will see the patient back in two weeks.
